stem: You are the paediatric registrar in a regional emergency department reviewing a 5-month-old previously well term girl on day 3 of coryza, cough and fast breathing. She is taking around 60% of her usual breastfeed volumes, has not vomited, and her last wet nappy was 3 hours ago. Initial observations showed mild subcostal recession with SpO2 92% in air; after suction of clear nasal secretions and a 90 mL breastfeed she is alert, smiling, with no recession at rest and SpO2 of 95% in air for 30 minutes. The mother is calm, has a working car, lives 25 minutes from the hospital, has a clear plan to seek review, and there is good GP follow-up the next morning.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 138, RR 44, SpO2 95% in air (after suction and feed), T 37.6 degrees C, capillary refill under 2 seconds
data_table.Examination: Mild wheeze with prolonged expiration, no chest wall recession at rest, good air entry, no focal crackles, mucous membranes moist
data_table.Investigations: Bedside nasopharyngeal aspirate viral PCR pending; no other investigations performed

lead_in: What is the most appropriate disposition?

options.text: Admit her for a chest x-ray and intravenous benzylpenicillin until viral nasopharyngeal aspirate PCR results return, given the fever and the possibility of an early bacterial lower respiratory tract co-infection
options.rationale: Routine chest imaging and empirical antibiotics are not indicated in uncomplicated bronchiolitis without focal signs or persistent high fever; both increase harm without altering outcomes in this clinical picture. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Discharge home with a written bronchiolitis action plan, simple analgesia for fever, advice on feed volumes and red-flag symptoms, and review by her general practitioner within 24 hours
options.rationale: She meets discharge criteria: SpO2 at or above 92% in air sustained for at least 30 minutes, mild work of breathing, feeding above 50% of baseline, no apnoeas, reliable carer and access to review; an action plan with red-flag advice and short-interval GP review is standard practice. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Admit her to the paediatric ward for nasogastric feeding at two-thirds maintenance and continuous pulse oximetry monitoring for at least 24 hours regardless of her current observations and ongoing feeding
options.rationale: Routine nasogastric feeding is reserved for infants taking under 50% of usual oral intake or in respiratory distress that precludes safe oral feeding, neither of which applies here. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Discharge her home only after a 4-hour high-flow nasal cannula trial at 2 L/kg/min in the ED short stay unit to confirm she does not develop any escalating oxygen requirement before going home
options.rationale: High-flow nasal cannula is an escalation therapy for infants with persistent hypoxia or severe work of breathing despite low-flow oxygen, not a pre-discharge screening test for a stable, normoxic infant. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Admit her for a trial of nebulised hypertonic 3% saline every 6 hours and a single inhaled salbutamol challenge to assess for any bronchodilator-responsive component of her wheeze before discharge
options.rationale: Nebulised hypertonic saline and trial salbutamol are not recommended in routine bronchiolitis management in infants under 12 months and do not change the disposition decision in a stable child meeting discharge criteria. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

explanation.summary: Disposition in bronchiolitis is driven by sustained SpO2, work of breathing, feeding adequacy, apnoeas and carer factors rather than by interventions of unproven benefit. This infant meets all RCH discharge criteria after simple suction and a feed. Hypertonic saline, bronchodilators, antibiotics and routine imaging are not indicated. Safe discharge requires a clear action plan and short-interval review.
key_points: Discharge thresholds: SpO2 at or above 92% in air sustained 30 minutes, feeds at or above 50% baseline, no apnoea, mild work of breathing
Hypertonic saline and salbutamol are not recommended in routine bronchiolitis under 12 months
Antibiotics and chest x-ray only with focal signs, persistent high fever or severe disease
Carer reliability, distance to care, and follow-up plan are part of the disposition decision
explanation.pearls: If SpO2 sits at 92% in air after suction and feed in an otherwise well infant with reliable carers, discharge with a written action plan and GP review within 24 hours is correct - admitting to escalate to high-flow is over-treatment.

stem: You are the GP registrar reviewing a 26-month-old non-Aboriginal boy with no significant past history brought in by his mother after 24 hours of irritability, ear-tugging and fever to 38.6 degrees C. He is otherwise feeding reasonably, drinking, has been playing intermittently between paracetamol doses and has had no vomiting. He is fully immunised including 13-valent pneumococcal conjugate vaccine, attends day care 3 days per week and has had no previous ear infections. Otoscopy shows a bulging, opaque right tympanic membrane with loss of landmarks and no perforation or discharge; the left ear is normal. There is no mastoid tenderness, neck stiffness, facial weakness, or coexisting bacterial focus.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 128, RR 28, SpO2 99% in air, T 38.6 degrees C, capillary refill under 2 seconds
data_table.Examination: Right TM bulging and opaque with loss of light reflex; left TM normal; no mastoid swelling; oropharynx mildly erythematous; chest clear

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial management of his ear?

options.text: Provide regular paracetamol and ibuprofen, written safety-net advice, and arrange routine review at 48 hours with antibiotics only if symptoms have worsened or have not improved at that review point
options.rationale: In a previously well, fully immunised non-Aboriginal child over 6 months with uncomplicated acute otitis media, watchful waiting with analgesia and 48-hour review is the preferred initial approach reserving antibiotics for failure to improve. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Acute otitis media)
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Acute otitis media

options.text: Commence oral amoxicillin-clavulanate 22.5 mg/kg twice daily for 7 days and refer urgently to ear, nose and throat outpatients for a discussion of grommet insertion if any further episodes occur
options.rationale: Amoxicillin-clavulanate is second-line if there has been recent amoxicillin or non-response, and grommet referral is for recurrent or persistent disease, not a first uncomplicated episode in a previously well child. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Acute otitis media)
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Acute otitis media

options.text: Commence oral amoxicillin 15 mg/kg three times daily for 5 days now and review only if he develops persistent fever, ear discharge or new symptoms over the next week of treatment
options.rationale: Immediate antibiotics are reserved for under-6-months, Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander children, bilateral disease under 2 years, perforation with discharge, or systemic illness; none apply to this otherwise well child meeting watchful-waiting criteria. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Acute otitis media)
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Acute otitis media

options.text: Arrange same-day ENT outpatient review and tympanocentesis for microbiological sampling and an audiology assessment before any decision is made about whether antibiotics or analgesia alone is most appropriate
options.rationale: Routine tympanocentesis and same-day ENT review are not indicated in uncomplicated first-episode acute otitis media; analgesia and 48-hour reassessment guide management at primary care level. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Acute otitis media)
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Acute otitis media

options.text: Refer him to the local emergency department for intravenous ceftriaxone, blood cultures and a lumbar puncture given the fever, irritability and unilateral bulging tympanic membrane on otoscopy today
options.rationale: He has no features of meningitis, sepsis or mastoiditis: a well child with a bulging tympanic membrane and low-grade fever does not require parenteral antibiotics, blood cultures or lumbar puncture. (Source: RCH CPG: Acute otitis media)

explanation.summary: Australian guidance supports watchful waiting in non-Aboriginal children over 6 months with uncomplicated, non-severe acute otitis media. Analgesia alone and review at 48 hours is the first step; antibiotics are reserved for at-risk groups, bilateral disease under 2 years, perforation with discharge, severe systemic illness, or failure to improve. Immediate amoxicillin would be appropriate in Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander children given the high rate of complicated otitis. Choosing parenteral antibiotics or ENT review is overreach in this clinical picture.
key_points: Watchful waiting with analgesia and 48-hour review for uncomplicated AOM in non-Aboriginal children over 6 months
Antibiotics from outset: under 6 months, Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander child, perforation with discharge, severe illness, or bilateral disease under 2 years
First-line antibiotic: amoxicillin; amoxicillin-clavulanate if non-response or recent amoxicillin
Tympanocentesis or parenteral antibiotics only if complicated (mastoiditis, sepsis, intracranial involvement)
explanation.pearls: In a non-Aboriginal, fully immunised, well-looking child over 6 months with first-episode unilateral AOM, regular paracetamol plus ibuprofen and 48-hour review beats amoxicillin from the door - antibiotics in this group reduce pain by less than a day and the number needed to treat is high.

sources.label: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Acute otitis media

stem: You are the paediatric ED registrar reviewing a 7-year-old boy with a known diagnosis of mild persistent asthma on a low-dose inhaled fluticasone preventer who presented with a 6-hour exacerbation triggered by an upper respiratory infection. On arrival he had moderate work of breathing with tracheal tug and SpO2 of 93% in air. He has received salbutamol 12 puffs by spacer at 20-minute intervals for three doses, ipratropium 8 puffs by spacer for three doses, and a single oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg dose 2 hours ago. He is now alert, talking in full sentences, with no recession, has just eaten and drunk, and is keen to go home; his salbutamol interval has been stretched to 3-hourly with no return of recession.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 104, RR 22, SpO2 97% in air, T 37.4 degrees C, capillary refill under 2 seconds
data_table.Examination: Mild scattered wheeze at end-expiration, no recession, full air entry bilaterally, no chest pain, no focal signs
data_table.Investigations: No imaging or bloods required; salbutamol response sustained at 3-hourly dosing across two cycles

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue 1-hourly salbutamol by spacer for a further 6 hours in the short stay unit and only consider discharge once the salbutamol interval can be stretched to at least every 6 hours during the day
options.rationale: A salbutamol interval of 3 to 4 hours sustained with stable observations is the accepted discharge threshold; insisting on a 6-hourly interval before discharge is more conservative than guidelines and prolongs ED length of stay without benefit. (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma acute)

options.text: Add a single oral montelukast 5 mg dose now, then a 5-day course at home in addition to his existing preventer, and discharge once 4 hours have elapsed from the last salbutamol given today
options.rationale: Routine addition of montelukast is not recommended for an acute exacerbation; preventer step-up is determined at outpatient review, not driven by a single ED episode in a child already on appropriate preventer therapy. (Source: National Asthma Council Australia: Asthma management handbook)
source_refs: National Asthma Council Australia: Asthma management handbook

options.text: Commence intravenous magnesium sulfate 50 mg/kg over 20 minutes and admit him to the paediatric high-dependency unit for ongoing monitoring of his magnesium level and his cardiac rhythm overnight
options.rationale: Intravenous magnesium is for severe or life-threatening exacerbations failing to respond to first-line therapy; this child has clinically improved and has no indication for magnesium or HDU admission. (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma acute)

options.text: Admit him to the paediatric ward for continuous pulse oximetry overnight and a 24-hour observation period before stepping down salbutamol given the duration of the symptoms and the upper respiratory tract trigger
options.rationale: Sustained 3-hourly salbutamol with stable saturations and minimal work of breathing supports safe discharge; admission for routine overnight observation in this scenario is overuse of inpatient resources. (Source: National Asthma Council Australia: Asthma management handbook)
source_refs: National Asthma Council Australia: Asthma management handbook

options.text: Discharge home with a written asthma action plan, oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg daily for a further 2 days, ongoing inhaled fluticasone preventer, and review by his GP within 48 to 72 hours of discharge
options.rationale: He meets discharge criteria: SpO2 above 94% in air, salbutamol stretched to at least 3-hourly with sustained response, no recession, and tolerating oral intake; a 3-day prednisolone course, written action plan and short-interval review complete the package. (Source: National Asthma Council Australia: Asthma management handbook)
source_refs: National Asthma Council Australia: Asthma management handbook

explanation.summary: Paediatric asthma discharge requires SpO2 above 94% in air, salbutamol stretched to at least 3-hourly with sustained effect, minimal work of breathing and tolerating oral intake. Discharge is paired with oral steroid completion, ongoing preventer, a written action plan and short-interval GP review. Admission, IV magnesium and montelukast are reserved for ongoing severity or unresolved features, not as routine. Over-prolonged ED stays add cost without clinical gain.
key_points: Discharge criteria: SpO2 above 94% air, salbutamol every 3 to 4 hours sustained, no recession, oral intake tolerated
Prednisolone 1 mg/kg daily for total 3 days in exacerbations needing systemic steroid
IV magnesium sulfate 50 mg/kg over 20 minutes is for severe or life-threatening attacks failing first-line therapy
Asthma action plan and GP review within 48 to 72 hours are mandatory on discharge
explanation.pearls: Stretching salbutamol to 3-hourly with no recession and SpO2 above 94% in air is the practical gate for safe discharge; do not wait for 6-hourly intervals or order IV magnesium for a child who is clinically improved.

sources.label: National Asthma Council Australia: Asthma management handbook

stem: You are the paediatric registrar reviewing a 22-month-old developmentally normal girl 90 minutes after a single 2-minute generalised tonic-clonic seizure that occurred at home during the rapid rise of a viral upper respiratory illness. The seizure self-terminated before paramedics arrived; she had a brief postictal drowsiness, then woke spontaneously and is now alert, smiling, playing and breastfeeding well. Examination identifies an obvious viral source with clear coryza and erythematous pharynx and reveals no focal neurological signs, no neck stiffness, no rash, and no significant past medical or family history other than a maternal aunt who had two febrile seizures in childhood with normal subsequent development.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 128, RR 26, SpO2 99% in air, T 39.1 degrees C (after one dose of paracetamol), capillary refill under 2 seconds
data_table.Examination: Alert, age-appropriate interaction, normal tone and reflexes, normal cranial nerves, soft anterior fontanelle, no rash, no neck stiffness, clear viral coryza
data_table.Investigations: No bloods, lumbar puncture, imaging or EEG performed

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Perform a lumbar puncture with cerebrospinal fluid microscopy, glucose and protein, blood cultures and commence empirical intravenous benzylpenicillin and ceftriaxone given the height of the fever today
options.rationale: Lumbar puncture after a simple febrile seizure is reserved for children under 6 months, those with persistent altered mental state, meningism, or who are partially treated with antibiotics; none of these apply here. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile seizure)

options.text: Admit her overnight for a 24-hour continuous video electroencephalogram and a paediatric neurology consultation to exclude an underlying focal epilepsy syndrome before discharging her home tomorrow
options.rationale: Inpatient video EEG and admission are not warranted after a single simple febrile seizure; her clinical picture and family history do not raise concern for an underlying epilepsy syndrome requiring inpatient workup. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile seizure)

options.text: Reassure the parents, give written advice on febrile seizure recurrence and basic first-aid recovery position management, discuss antipyretic use for comfort, and discharge home with GP follow-up
options.rationale: She has a simple febrile seizure with a clear viral focus and a normal neurological examination; routine investigations and admission are not indicated and a structured parental education and safety-net discharge is correct. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile seizure)

options.text: Commence regular sodium valproate as a maintenance anti-seizure medication for the next 6 months given the family history of febrile seizures in a first-degree relative and the height of the documented fever
options.rationale: Maintenance anti-seizure medication after simple febrile seizures does not reduce future febrile or unprovoked seizures and carries significant adverse effects; it is not recommended for prevention after a first event. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile seizure)

options.text: Arrange a non-contrast CT brain scan today, an outpatient electroencephalogram within a week and outpatient paediatric neurology review before any decision is made about discharge home this afternoon
options.rationale: Routine neuroimaging and EEG are not indicated after a single simple febrile seizure in a developmentally normal child with a normal neurological examination and an obvious infective source. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile seizure)

explanation.summary: Simple febrile seizures last under 15 minutes, are generalised, and are not followed by focal neurology or recurrence within 24 hours. In a developmentally normal child older than 6 months with an identifiable febrile source and a normal examination, routine workup is not required. Counselling covers recurrence risk (about 30%), reassurance that simple febrile seizures do not cause brain injury or developmental harm, basic first-aid, and that antipyretics do not prevent recurrence. Maintenance anti-seizure medication after simple febrile seizures is not recommended.
key_points: Simple febrile seizure: under 15 minutes, generalised, not recurrent within 24 hours, normal neuro exam
Recurrence risk around 30%, higher with first event under 18 months or low fever threshold
Lumbar puncture if under 6 months, meningism, partially treated with antibiotics or persistent altered mental state
No role for maintenance anti-seizure medication or routine EEG/imaging after simple febrile seizure
explanation.pearls: Antipyretics provide comfort but do not reduce the risk of seizure recurrence; written parental advice on duration, recovery position, and when to call for help is the highest-yield intervention you offer at discharge.

subtopic_detail: Intussusception recognition and air enema indication
stem: You are the paediatric surgical registrar called to ED to review an 11-month-old boy with 18 hours of intermittent severe screaming attacks, drawing his knees up and going pale, each lasting 5 minutes and recurring every 20 to 30 minutes. He has vomited four times in the last 6 hours (now bilious), refused his last two feeds, and passed a small amount of dark blood mixed with mucus per rectum on the way to hospital. On examination he is sleepy between episodes but rousable, has a soft non-distended abdomen with a palpable sausage-shaped mass in the right upper quadrant, and his peripheries are well perfused. He has had no surgery or foreign-body ingestion.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 156, RR 36, SpO2 98% in air, T 37.2 degrees C, BP 96/58, capillary refill 2 seconds
data_table.Examination: Sleepy between episodes, mildly dehydrated, abdomen soft with right upper quadrant sausage mass, no peritonism, blood-stained mucus on PR
data_table.Investigations: Urgent abdominal ultrasound shows a target sign 3.5 cm diameter at the ileocolic junction, no free fluid, no perforation; venous gas pH 7.34, lactate 1.8 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Admit him for nil by mouth, intravenous maintenance fluids and serial abdominal examinations over the next 12 hours, repeating the ultrasound only if his symptoms have not spontaneously settled in that period
options.rationale: Watchful waiting risks bowel ischaemia and perforation; ultrasound-confirmed intussusception with classic clinical features warrants prompt air enema reduction rather than observation. (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception)

options.text: Insert an intravenous cannula, commence a 20 mL/kg sodium chloride 0.9% bolus, place a nasogastric tube on free drainage, give intravenous antibiotics and proceed to a radiologist-led air enema reduction with surgical standby
options.rationale: Confirmed ileocolic intussusception without perforation or peritonism is managed with resuscitation, decompression, intravenous antibiotic cover and image-guided air enema reduction performed with surgical standby in case of perforation or failed reduction. (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception)

options.text: Transfer him directly to the operating theatre for an immediate diagnostic laparoscopy and manual reduction with possible bowel resection given the bilious vomiting and the blood per rectum noted on the way to hospital
options.rationale: Primary operative reduction is reserved for failed enema reduction, peritonism, perforation or shock; he has none of these and a non-operative air enema is the first-line therapy with high success rates. (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception)

options.text: Order a non-contrast computed tomography of the abdomen and pelvis to confirm the ultrasound finding and exclude a lead point before proceeding with any decision about enema reduction or surgical referral
options.rationale: Ultrasound is the diagnostic modality of choice in paediatric intussusception with high sensitivity and specificity; routine CT adds ionising radiation without changing management. (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception)

options.text: Give a stat dose of intramuscular morphine for the colicky pain and observe him in the short stay unit for 4 hours, expecting many ileocolic intussusceptions to reduce spontaneously without intervention
options.rationale: Spontaneous reduction is unreliable and delays risk ischaemic complications; analgesia is appropriate but does not replace urgent air enema or surgical reduction. (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception)

explanation.summary: Classic intussusception in an infant under 2 years presents with paroxysmal pain, vomiting (often bilious late), and redcurrant jelly stool; ultrasound shows a target or doughnut sign. Without peritonism, perforation or shock, management is resuscitation, decompression, intravenous antibiotic cover, and image-guided air enema reduction with surgical standby. Operative reduction is reserved for failed enema, peritonism, perforation, or shock. CT adds nothing in classic ileocolic disease and should be avoided.
key_points: Air enema reduction success rate approaches 80 to 90% in uncomplicated ileocolic intussusception
Absolute contraindications to enema: perforation, peritonism, shock; relative: persistent ileus, lead point identified
Bilious vomiting in an infant should always prompt urgent surgical assessment
Recurrence after air enema reduction occurs in around 10%, usually within 48 hours, and a second air enema is acceptable if no peritonism
explanation.pearls: Surgical standby during the air enema is non-negotiable: if perforation occurs during reduction or the enema fails, operative reduction must follow within minutes - book the radiologist and theatre in parallel, not sequentially.

sources.label: Therapeutic Guidelines: Surgical - Paediatric abdominal emergencies
